title: Django Intro
content:
Django Introduction

The Python Web Framework

Django is a free and open-source Python web framework that makes it significantly easier to build backend web applications. It includes built-in tools for handling URLs, views, templates, models, and databases right out of the box.



Unlike traditional frameworks, Django follows the strict Model-View-Template (MVT) architecture to encourage clean design, rapid development, and highly scalable dynamic websites and web APIs.

title: Virtual Env
content:
Django Virtual Environment

Package Isolation

Before starting a Django project, it’s highly recommended to create a dedicated "virtual environment" for package isolation. This ensures that the libraries you install for this specific project do not conflict with other Python projects on your computer.



Terminal Commands
# 1. Create the virtual environment named 'myworld'
python -m venv myworld

# 2. Activate it (Windows)
myworld\Scripts\activate.bat

# OR Activate it (Mac/Linux)
source myworld/bin/activate

Once activated, your terminal prompt will change, and you can safely install Django within this isolated environment.

title: Install Django
content:
Install Django

The pip Package Manager

With your virtual environment successfully activated, you use Python's built-in package manager (pip) to download and install the Django framework directly from the internet.

Installation Command
# Install the framework
pip install django

# Verify the installation was successful
django-admin --version

title: Create Project
content:
Create Django Project

Starting the Engine

After installing Django, you initialize a new project using the django-admin utility. This command generates the core folder structure required to run a Python web server.

Project Initialization
# Create the core project folder
django-admin startproject my_tennis_club

# Navigate into the new folder
cd my_tennis_club

# Start the local development server to test it!
python manage.py runserver

Once the server is running, you can open your web browser to http://127.0.0.1:8000/ to see the default Django welcome page!

title: Create App
content:
Create Django App

Project Components

In Django, a "Project" is the entire website, while an "App" is a specific component that encapsulates functionality—such as a blog, a user management system, or a members list. One project can contain many apps!



Generating an App
# Inside your project folder, run this to generate a new app:
python manage.py startapp members

This creates a new folder containing files like views.py and models.py, which you will use to build the specific logic for that part of your application.

title: Views
content:
Django Views

The Application Logic

A Django view is a Python function (or class) that receives a web request from a user and returns a web response. The response can be the HTML contents of a web page, a redirect, or an error (like a 404).

views.py
from django.http import HttpResponse

# A simple view function
def members(request):
    return HttpResponse("Hello world! Welcome to the members area.")

Views define your core application logic and are usually located inside the views.py file of your specific app.

title: URLs
content:
Django URLs

Routing Requests

How does Django know which View to run when a user visits a specific webpage? It uses the URL configuration (URLconf) to map web request paths to view functions.



urls.py
from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    # When a user visits '/members/', trigger the views.members function!
    path('members/', views.members, name='members'),
]

In your app or project urls.py, you add path patterns that connect specific URLs to views so that when a user visits a URL, the appropriate Python code handles the request.

title: Templates
content:
Django Templates

Dynamic HTML

Django Templates are HTML files that display dynamic content in your web pages. You create a templates folder in your app directory and store HTML files there.



Use template tags and variables inside double curly braces ({{ }}) to insert data passed from your Python views directly into your HTML.

Template Syntax
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<body>
  <!-- The variable inside the braces will be replaced by actual data! -->
  <h1>Welcome back, {{ firstname }}!</h1>
</body>
</html>

title: Models
content:
Django Models

Database Mapping

A Django Model represents a single database table. You define models in your app’s models.py file using standard Python classes. Each class attribute corresponds to a database field (column).



models.py
from django.db import models

# This Python class will become a database table!
class Member(models.Model):
  firstname = models.CharField(max_length=255)
  lastname = models.CharField(max_length=255)
  joined_date = models.DateField(null=True)

After defining models, you run "migrations" (using python manage.py makemigrations and python manage.py migrate) to instruct Django to create the actual SQL database tables for you automatically.

title: Insert Data
content:
Insert Data in Django

Creating Records

Once a model is defined and tables are created via migrations, you can insert data into the database straight from your Django views or the Django shell.

Saving Objects
from .models import Member

# 1. Create a new instance of the Member model
new_member = Member(firstname='Alice', lastname='Smith')

# 2. Call save() to write it to the database!
new_member.save()

Using the model’s ORM (Object-Relational Mapper) lets you add members or other objects programmatically without writing a single line of SQL.

title: Update & Delete
content:
Update and Delete Data

Modifying Records

With Django models, you can retrieve objects from the database, change their fields, and save them back to update records. You can also destroy objects completely. This gives you full CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) capability directly from Python code.

Full CRUD Operations
from .models import Member

# --- UPDATE ---
# Fetch a specific member, change their name, and save
user = Member.objects.get(id=1)
user.firstname = 'Bob'
user.save()

# --- DELETE ---
# Fetch another member and permanently delete them
bad_user = Member.objects.get(id=2)
bad_user.delete()

title: Admin Interface
content:
Django Admin Interface

The Ultimate Time-Saver

Django comes with a powerful built-in admin interface that lets you manage your models through a beautiful browser UI. You don't have to build a dashboard from scratch!



admin.py
from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Member

# Register your model to make it visible in the Admin Panel
admin.site.register(Member)

After enabling the admin site in your project’s urls.py and registering your model, you can access this CRUD interface at /admin/. It allows users with admin privileges to view, add, edit, and delete database records effortlessly.

title: QuerySets
content:
Django QuerySets (Filtering & Ordering)

Fetching Data

A QuerySet in Django is a collection of data from the database obtained via the model’s ORM. QuerySets let you retrieve all records or filter them efficiently before passing them to your templates.

Filters and Ordering
from .models import Member

# Get every single member in the database
all_members = Member.objects.all()

# Get only members named 'Alice'
alices = Member.objects.filter(firstname='Alice')

# Get all members, but order them alphabetically by last name
sorted_members = Member.objects.all().order_by('lastname')

You can also use values() or values_list() to customize the format of returned data. QuerySets make it easy to extract exactly the data you need for a view or template.

title: Static Files
content:
Django Add Static Files

Styling the Web

Static files like CSS, JavaScript, and images are used to style and add interactivity to your web pages. In Django, you place these files inside a static folder in your app or project structure.

Loading Static Assets
<!-- 1. You MUST load the static tag at the very top of your HTML file -->
{% load static %}

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <!-- 2. Use the tag to generate the correct server path -->
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="{% static 'css/style.css' %}">
</head>
<body>
  <h1>Styled with Django!</h1>
</body>
</html>

This lets you serve custom styles and scripts for your application cleanly.

title: WhiteNoise
content:
Django Install WhiteNoise

Production Serving

By default, Django is not built to serve static files in a live production environment (it assumes you will use a dedicated server like Nginx). WhiteNoise is a Python package that solves this by serving static files directly from the Django application.



Installing WhiteNoise
# Install the package via terminal
pip install whitenoise

# Then, add it to your MIDDLEWARE list in settings.py:
MIDDLEWARE = [
    'django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware',
    'whitenoise.middleware.WhiteNoiseMiddleware', # <-- Add this right after Security
    # ... other middleware
]

After installing WhiteNoise, your static files are served efficiently even when DEBUG is set to False. This completely simplifies deployment.

title: Collect Static
content:
Django Collect Static Files

Gathering Assets

In production, Django needs all static files from every single app to be collected into one single master directory. You define this master folder using STATIC_ROOT in settings.py.



The Collect Command
# Run this in your terminal right before deploying to a live server:
python manage.py collectstatic

This command automatically gathers all static assets from various apps into the central folder. This makes your static assets easy to serve with WhiteNoise or a CDN.

title: Global Static
content:
Global Static Files

Project-Wide Assets

You can also manage static files that span across the entire project (not just within a single app, like a global company logo or a main CSS reset file) by configuring Django to look in custom directories for static assets.

settings.py Configuration
import os
from pathlib import Path

BASE_DIR = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent

# Tell Django where to look for global static files
STATICFILES_DIRS = [
    os.path.join(BASE_DIR, 'static'),
]

This allows CSS, JS, or images to be shared easily among multiple apps in your project.

title: Adding JS
content:
Adding JS Files in Django

Frontend Interactivity

To include JavaScript in your Django pages, you place your JS files inside the static folder just like CSS files. This allows you to add dynamic front-end behaviors like click handlers and animations to your server-rendered templates.

Loading JavaScript
{% load static %}

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<body>
  <h1>Interactive Dashboard</h1>
  <button id="myBtn">Click Me!</button>

  <!-- Load your JS file right before the closing body tag -->
  <script src="{% static 'js/main.js' %}"></script>
</body>
</html>

title: PostgreSQL Intro
content:
Django PostgreSQL Introduction

Production Databases

PostgreSQL is a powerful open-source relational database system that Django supports natively through its ORM. Unlike the default SQLite database (which is just a file on your computer), PostgreSQL is suited for high-performance and production-scale apps.



In this section, you will learn how to create a PostgreSQL database on AWS RDS, connect your Django app to it, and use it to store your model data securely in the cloud.

title: AWS RDS Setup
content:
Create AWS Account for PostgreSQL

Cloud Infrastructure

To set up a PostgreSQL database for your Django app, you first need an AWS account. AWS offers Relational Database Service (RDS), where you can create a PostgreSQL instance and configure its database name, master user, and password.

This database can then be connected to your Django project via the DATABASES setting in your settings.py file.

title: Connect DB
content:
Connect Django to PostgreSQL

Configuring settings.py

Once your PostgreSQL database is ready on AWS RDS, you must update your Django settings.py file to use the PostgreSQL database engine instead of the default SQLite.

Database Configuration
DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',
        'NAME': 'your_db_name',
        'USER': 'your_master_username',
        'PASSWORD': 'your_password',
        'HOST': 'your-rds-endpoint.amazonaws.com',
        'PORT': '5432',
    }
}

You will specify the database name, user, password, host, and port so Django can communicate with PostgreSQL. After this configuration, run python manage.py migrate to create your tables in the cloud!

title: Cloud Data
content:
Add Members using PostgreSQL

Seamless ORM Integration

With PostgreSQL successfully connected, you can insert data through the Django ORM, the admin interface, or your application views exactly as you did before.

Django handles the SQL translation automatically. This ensures that all model records are securely stored in the PostgreSQL backend database. It’s a highly reliable and scalable option for production data compared to the default SQLite database used in local development.

title: Elastic Beanstalk
content:
Deploy Django with Elastic Beanstalk

Going Live

Elastic Beanstalk (EB) is an AWS service that lets you deploy web applications easily without needing to manually manage the underlying server infrastructure (like EC2 instances and Nginx routing).



In this deployment phase, you prepare your app for Elastic Beanstalk and then create and launch an application environment through the AWS console or the EB CLI. This makes your Django site accessible on the public internet.

title: Deploy Configs
content:
Project Deployment Files

Preparation

To deploy using Elastic Beanstalk, you must first create a requirements.txt file listing all of your Python dependencies by running pip freeze > requirements.txt in your terminal.

.ebextensions folder
# Create a folder named .ebextensions in your project root
# Inside it, create a file named django.config:

option_settings:
  aws:elasticbeanstalk:container:python:
    WSGIPath: my_project_name.wsgi:application

This django.config file configures the WSGI application entry point. These files help EB know exactly how to set up and run your Django application on their servers.

title: Deploy & Update
content:
Deploy and Update Django App

Pushing to Production

After preparing your deployment files, you create a ZIP file of your Django project that includes your code and the .ebextensions folder, then upload it directly to the Elastic Beanstalk console.

EB automatically creates the environment, provisions the servers, and deploys your application! For future updates, simply create a new ZIP with any code changes and upload it again to seamlessly update the live site with zero downtime.

title: Final Summary
content:
Django Final Summary 🚀

Backend Mastered! 🏆

This concludes your Django tutorial. Django is a powerful and open-source Python web framework that follows the Model-View-Template (MVT) design pattern, making it easy to build scalable and dynamic backend web applications.



Throughout this tutorial, you learned to:

  • Set up a Django development environment and install Django.
  • Create Django projects and apps.
  • Handle HTTP requests and define views.
  • Set up URL routing to link views and web pages.
  • Use templates for dynamic HTML using Django’s template engine.
  • Define database models and perform CRUD operations with Django’s ORM.
  • Use the built-in admin interface to manage data in the browser.
  • Work with QuerySets to filter and order data.
  • Manage static files such as CSS, JavaScript, and images.
  • Connect Django to a production-ready PostgreSQL database.
  • Deploy your Django application to the web using AWS Elastic Beanstalk.

With these skills, you are now equipped to build complete web applications—from setup to deployment. Continue exploring Django’s rich ecosystem, including forms, REST APIs, middleware, and authentication, to deepen your backend development expertise.

Congratulations on completing the Django tutorial!
